title: Unlocking Earth's Secrets with Seismic Waves

passages.title: Unlocking Earth's Secrets with Seismic Waves
passages.text:
1 Imagine standing on Earth's surface and wanting to know what lies thousands of miles below your feet. Unlike oceans, forests, or mountains, Earth's deepest layers cannot be directly explored. The deepest holes humans have drilled reach only a small fraction of the distance to Earth's center. Yet scientists have developed a way to investigate Earth's interior without ever traveling there. They use seismic waves generated by earthquakes.

2 When an earthquake occurs, energy spreads outward from the source in all directions. This energy travels through Earth as seismic waves. Scientists detect these waves using instruments called seismographs, which record the movement of the ground. By examining how seismic waves travel through the planet, researchers can gather information about materials hidden deep below the surface.

3 Seismic waves do not all behave the same way. One type, called a primary wave or P-wave, travels very quickly and can move through solids and liquids. Another type, called a secondary wave or S-wave, moves more slowly and can travel only through solids. These differences have provided important clues about the structure of Earth.

4 As seismic waves move through the planet, they encounter layers made of different materials. Some layers are denser than others. When waves pass from one material into another, they may change speed or bend. Scientists call this process refraction. By studying these changes, researchers can estimate the composition and properties of the layers through which the waves travel.

5 In the early twentieth century, scientists noticed a puzzling pattern. After some earthquakes, S-waves failed to appear at certain locations around the world. These regions became known as S-wave shadow zones. Since S-waves cannot travel through liquids, scientists concluded that a large liquid layer must exist deep inside Earth.

6 P-waves provided additional evidence. Although P-waves can travel through liquids, they slow down and change direction when they enter liquid material. Scientists observed that P-waves created their own shadow zones where fewer waves were detected. This discovery supported the idea that Earth contains a liquid outer core.

7 Further analysis of seismic waves revealed that Earth is divided into several layers. The outermost layer is the crust, which forms the continents and ocean floors. Beneath the crust lies the mantle, a thick layer of hot rock. Below the mantle is the outer core, which consists mostly of liquid iron and nickel. At the center of the planet is the inner core, a solid sphere made primarily of iron.

8 Scientists once questioned how the inner core could be solid when temperatures near Earth's center are extremely high. Seismic waves helped answer this question. Researchers found that some P-waves travel through the inner core differently than they travel through the liquid outer core. These observations showed that the immense pressure at Earth's center is great enough to keep the inner core solid despite the heat.

9 Today, scientists use data from thousands of earthquakes each year to create detailed models of Earth's interior. Advanced computers analyze seismic wave patterns and help researchers refine their understanding of the planet. The process is similar to how doctors use imaging technology to examine structures inside the human body.

10 Seismic waves have transformed the study of Earth. Although people cannot directly observe most of the planet's interior, the behavior of these waves has allowed scientists to identify Earth's layers and determine many of their properties. Each earthquake provides another opportunity to learn about the hidden world beneath Earth's surface.

title: The Moving Puzzle Beneath Our Feet

passages.title: The Moving Puzzle Beneath Our Feet
passages.text:
1 Most people think of the ground beneath their feet as solid and unchanging. Roads, buildings, and mountains often appear permanent. However, Earth's surface is constantly moving, even if the movement is too slow for people to notice. Scientists have discovered that the outer layer of Earth is broken into large pieces called tectonic plates. These plates slowly shift over time, changing the planet's surface in powerful ways.

2 The outermost part of Earth is called the lithosphere. It includes Earth's crust and the uppermost portion of the mantle. Rather than forming one continuous shell, the lithosphere is divided into several major tectonic plates and many smaller plates. These plates fit together somewhat like pieces of a giant puzzle covering the planet.

3 Although tectonic plates appear stable, they are constantly moving. Most plates move only a few centimeters each year. That rate may seem slow, but over millions of years, the movement can significantly reshape Earth's surface. Scientists have measured plate motion using satellites and other advanced technologies. Their observations show that plates move in different directions and at different speeds.

4 The movement of tectonic plates is driven by processes occurring deep within Earth. Beneath the lithosphere lies a layer called the asthenosphere. The rock in this layer is extremely hot and behaves somewhat like a slow-moving fluid. Heat from Earth's interior causes material within the asthenosphere to rise, cool, and sink in a continuous cycle. These movements help push and pull the tectonic plates above them.

5 Tectonic plates interact at their boundaries, where the edges of plates meet. Scientists have identified three main types of plate boundaries. At divergent boundaries, plates move away from one another. At convergent boundaries, plates move toward one another. At transform boundaries, plates slide past each other horizontally.

6 Divergent boundaries can create new sections of Earth's crust. One example is the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, a massive underwater mountain range that runs through the Atlantic Ocean. There, molten rock rises from below Earth's surface and cools, forming new crust as the plates gradually move apart. This process is known as seafloor spreading.

7 Convergent boundaries often produce dramatic geological features. When two continental plates collide, the crust can buckle and fold, creating mountain ranges. The Himalayas, which include Mount Everest, formed when the Indian Plate collided with the Eurasian Plate millions of years ago. In other cases, an oceanic plate may slide beneath a continental plate in a process called subduction. Subduction can lead to the formation of volcanoes and deep ocean trenches.

8 Transform boundaries create a different type of interaction. As plates grind past each other, stress can build up along faults in Earth's crust. When the stress becomes too great, the rocks suddenly shift, releasing energy in the form of an earthquake. California's San Andreas Fault is one of the best-known examples of a transform boundary.

9 Evidence supporting plate movement comes from several sources. Scientists have observed matching fossils on continents separated by oceans. Similar rock formations have also been found on distant continents. In addition, measurements taken by satellites confirm that tectonic plates continue to move today. Together, these observations support the theory of plate tectonics, which explains how Earth's surface changes over time.

10 The movement of tectonic plates affects nearly every part of Earth's surface. Mountain ranges rise, oceans expand, volcanoes erupt, and earthquakes occur because of interactions between plates. Although these changes usually happen slowly, they play a major role in shaping the world people live in today.

11 Scientists continue to study tectonic plates to better understand Earth's past and predict future geological activity. By learning how plates move and interact, researchers gain valuable insights into the forces that have shaped the planet for billions of years. The ground beneath our feet may seem still, but it is actually part of a constantly moving system that is reshaping Earth one small movement at a time.

title: Building Earth's Surface

passages.title: Building Earth's Surface
passages.text:
1 Earth’s surface is constantly changing. While some changes happen quickly, many occur so slowly that they are difficult to notice during a human lifetime. One group of forces, known as constructive geologic processes, creates new landforms and adds material to Earth's surface. These processes have helped shape continents, mountains, islands, and other features found around the world.

2 One of the most powerful constructive processes is volcanic activity. Deep within Earth, molten rock called magma forms beneath the surface. When pressure builds, magma can rise through cracks in the crust and erupt as lava. As the lava cools and hardens, it creates new rock. Repeated eruptions can gradually build mountains and even entire islands.

3 The Hawaiian Islands provide an example of how volcanic activity can create land. Over millions of years, lava flowing from underwater volcanoes accumulated layer upon layer. Eventually, some of these volcanic peaks rose above the ocean's surface, forming islands. Today, the Hawaiian Islands continue to grow as volcanic activity adds new rock to the landscape.

4 Mountain building is another constructive process. Tectonic plates constantly move across Earth's surface. When two continental plates collide, enormous forces push sections of the crust upward. Over long periods of time, these collisions create mountain ranges. The Himalayas, which contain some of the tallest mountains on Earth, formed through this process and continue to rise today.

5 Constructive processes can also occur when rivers deposit sediment. Sediment consists of small pieces of rock, sand, and soil carried by moving water. When a river slows down, it may drop some of this material. Over time, layers of sediment can accumulate and create new landforms. River deltas, such as the Mississippi River Delta, formed when deposited sediment built up near the mouths of rivers.

6 Scientists continue to study constructive processes because they help explain how Earth's surface has changed throughout history. Although these processes often take thousands or millions of years, they have played a major role in creating many of the landscapes people see today.

passages.title: Wearing Earth's Surface Away
passages.text:
1 While some geologic processes build up Earth's surface, others gradually break it down. These destructive geologic processes remove, wear away, or transport materials from one location to another. Together, they constantly reshape the planet.

2 One common destructive process is weathering. Weathering occurs when rocks break into smaller pieces. This can happen when water freezes inside cracks and expands, causing rocks to split apart. Plant roots can also grow into cracks and slowly force rocks to break. Over time, weathering reduces large rocks into smaller fragments.

3 Once rock and soil have been broken apart, erosion can move the material to new locations. Water, wind, ice, and gravity are major causes of erosion. Flowing rivers can carry sediment downstream, while strong winds can transport sand across deserts. Glaciers slowly scrape and remove rock as they move across the land.

4 The Grand Canyon demonstrates the power of erosion. Over millions of years, the Colorado River cut through layers of rock, gradually carving the canyon. What began as a relatively level landscape was transformed into one of the most recognizable landforms in North America.

5 Destructive processes can sometimes occur rapidly. During powerful storms, floodwaters may wash away soil and reshape riverbanks in just a few hours. Coastal cliffs can also collapse as waves continually erode their bases. Although these changes may seem sudden, they result from the same natural forces that slowly shape Earth's surface over longer periods.

6 Constructive and destructive processes often work together. Material removed from one location through erosion may later be deposited somewhere else, helping create new landforms. As a result, Earth's surface is constantly being reshaped by a balance of forces that build up and wear down the land.

7 Scientists study destructive processes to better understand how landscapes change over time. By observing weathering, erosion, and deposition, researchers can explain many of the features found on Earth's surface and predict how they may continue to change in the future.

Earth's surface is continually reshaped by the combined effects of constructive and destructive processes.

title: A Story Written in Stone

passages.title: A Story Written in Stone
passages.text:
1 Earth’s surface may seem permanent, but it has changed dramatically over time. Mountains have risen and worn away, oceans have covered land that is now dry, and living things have appeared, changed, and disappeared. Scientists study the geologic record to find evidence of these changes. The geologic record includes rock layers, fossils, and other clues preserved in Earth’s crust.

2 Sedimentary rock is especially useful for understanding Earth’s past. These rocks form when layers of sediment, such as sand, mud, and tiny pieces of once-living things, build up over time. As more layers collect, pressure turns the sediment into rock. In many places, the oldest layers are found near the bottom, while younger layers are closer to the top.

3 Rock layers can reveal how an environment changed. For example, a layer of sandstone may show that an area was once covered by a beach or desert. A layer containing shells from sea creatures may suggest that the same area was once underwater. If scientists find coal, they may infer that the area was once a swampy environment filled with plants.

4 Fossils provide another important part of the geologic record. A fossil is the preserved evidence of a plant, animal, or other organism from the past. Fossils may include bones, shells, footprints, leaf prints, or burrows. By studying fossils, scientists can learn what kinds of organisms lived in an area and what the environment was like when they were alive.

5 Sometimes, the geologic record shows that Earth’s surface changed gradually. A thick stack of sedimentary layers may represent millions of years of deposition. Other evidence points to sudden events. A layer of volcanic ash, for example, may show that a volcanic eruption once covered an area. A jumbled layer of broken rock may suggest a landslide, flood, or earthquake.

6 The geologic record is not always complete. Some rock layers are destroyed by erosion, melted by heat, or changed by pressure. Even so, the evidence that remains helps scientists piece together Earth’s history. Like detectives, they use clues in rocks and fossils to reconstruct the changing surface of the planet.

passages.title: Fossils from an Ancient Sea
passages.text:
1 In parts of Ohio, visitors can find fossils of sea creatures in rocks far from any ocean. At first, this may seem surprising. Today, Ohio is covered by forests, farms, rivers, towns, and cities. However, the fossils preserved in its rocks reveal that the region looked very different hundreds of millions of years ago.

2 Many Ohio fossils come from the Paleozoic Era, a long span of geologic time when shallow seas covered large parts of what is now North America. In those seas lived animals such as trilobites, brachiopods, corals, and crinoids. When these organisms died, some were buried by sediment on the seafloor. Over time, the sediment hardened into rock, preserving evidence of the ancient sea life.

3 These fossils help scientists understand past environments. Coral fossils, for example, suggest warm, shallow marine waters. Brachiopods and crinoids also indicate that ocean water once covered the area. Finding these fossils in Ohio provides evidence that Earth’s surface has changed greatly over time. Land that is now far from the coast was once part of a sea.

4 Rock layers in Ohio also show that the environment did not remain the same forever. Some layers formed from mud deposited in quiet water. Others formed from sand carried by stronger currents. Changes in the type of rock suggest changes in water depth, shoreline position, or sediment supply. These details help scientists track how ancient environments shifted.

5 The fossils do more than show where oceans once existed. They also help scientists compare the ages of rock layers in different places. If the same type of fossil is found in two separate rock layers, scientists may infer that the layers formed during a similar time period. This allows researchers to connect pieces of the geologic record across large distances.

6 Ohio’s ancient sea fossils are reminders that Earth is dynamic. The surface people see today is only one chapter in a much longer story. By studying fossils and rock layers, scientists can find evidence of oceans, changing environments, and life from the distant past.

title: The Science of Staying in Motion

passages.title: The Science of Staying in Motion
passages.text:
1 Imagine riding your bicycle down a hill. At first, you may pedal to gain speed. Then, as the hill becomes steeper, the bicycle moves faster even without additional pedaling. Eventually, you reach the bottom and squeeze the brakes to slow down. This simple ride demonstrates an important idea in physics: forces can change the motion of objects.

2 A force is a push or a pull acting on an object. Forces are responsible for many of the motions people observe every day. Whether a soccer player kicks a ball, a student opens a door, or a rocket launches into space, forces are involved. Scientists study forces to understand why objects move, stop, speed up, slow down, or change direction.

3 Motion describes an object's change in position over time. An object may move quickly, slowly, or remain at rest. However, an object's motion will not change unless a force acts on it. This idea was explained by Sir Isaac Newton in his First Law of Motion. The law states that an object at rest tends to stay at rest, and an object in motion tends to remain in motion unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.

4 Consider a hockey puck sliding across smooth ice. Once the puck is struck, it continues moving in the same direction. If there were no opposing forces, the puck would keep moving indefinitely. In reality, friction between the puck and the ice gradually slows it down. Friction is a force that opposes motion when two surfaces come into contact.

5 Friction plays an important role in everyday life. Without friction, people would struggle to walk because their shoes could not grip the ground. Cars rely on friction between tires and roads to move safely. Although friction often slows moving objects, it also helps people control motion.

6 Gravity is another force that affects motion. Gravity is the force that pulls objects toward one another. On Earth, gravity pulls objects toward the planet's center. When a person drops a pencil, gravity causes it to fall. Gravity also keeps the Moon in orbit around Earth and helps hold Earth's atmosphere in place.

7 Sometimes multiple forces act on an object at the same time. When forces are balanced, they cancel each other out, and the object's motion does not change. For example, a book resting on a table experiences the downward pull of gravity and the upward support force from the table. Because these forces are balanced, the book remains still.

8 When forces are unbalanced, motion changes. A soccer ball sitting on a field will remain at rest until a player kicks it. The force from the kick causes the ball to accelerate, or change its velocity. Velocity includes both speed and direction. As a result, the ball may move faster, slower, or in a different direction than before.

9 Engineers and scientists apply their understanding of forces in many ways. Automobile designers use knowledge of friction and motion to improve vehicle safety. Aerospace engineers calculate forces when designing airplanes and spacecraft. Even athletes use an understanding of forces to improve their performance in sports.

10 Forces may be invisible, but their effects can be seen everywhere. From a bicycle speeding downhill to a spacecraft traveling through space, forces influence how objects move. By studying pushes, pulls, gravity, and friction, scientists continue to develop technologies and explain the motion of objects throughout the universe.

title: The Invisible Forces Around Us

passages.title: The Invisible Forces Around Us
passages.text:
1 Imagine dropping a set of keys and watching them fall to the ground. Later, you place a magnet near a paper clip and see the paper clip move without being touched. You might even rub a balloon on your hair and notice that it sticks to a wall. These events seem very different, but they have something in common. In each case, an invisible field creates a force that acts on an object.

2 A force is a push or a pull that can change an object's motion. Many people think forces require direct contact, such as kicking a soccer ball or pushing a shopping cart. However, some forces can act from a distance. These forces are produced by fields that extend through the space around certain objects.

3 One of the most familiar examples is the gravitational field. Every object with mass creates a gravitational field around itself. The larger an object's mass, the stronger its gravitational field tends to be. Earth's gravitational field pulls objects toward the planet's center, which is why dropped objects fall to the ground.

4 Gravity affects much more than objects on Earth's surface. The Moon remains in orbit around Earth because Earth's gravitational field pulls on it. At the same time, the Moon's gravity affects Earth, causing ocean tides. The Sun's enormous gravitational field helps keep Earth and the other planets moving in predictable paths around it.

5 Another type of field is a magnetic field. Magnets create magnetic fields that can attract or repel certain materials. Iron, nickel, and cobalt are examples of metals that are strongly affected by magnetic fields. When a paper clip moves toward a magnet, the magnet's field exerts a force on the paper clip even though the two objects are not touching.

6 Magnetic fields play an important role in modern technology. Electric motors, speakers, and magnetic storage devices all rely on magnetic forces. Earth itself produces a magnetic field that extends far into space. This field helps protect the planet from harmful particles traveling from the Sun.

7 Electrostatic fields create another type of force. Electrostatic forces result from electric charges. Objects can become positively or negatively charged when electrons move from one object to another. Opposite charges attract, while like charges repel.

8 A common example of electrostatic force occurs when a balloon is rubbed against hair. During this process, electrons transfer between the two materials, creating an imbalance of charge. The charged balloon can then attract lightweight objects or cling to a wall because of electrostatic forces.

9 Although gravitational, magnetic, and electrostatic fields are different, they share an important characteristic. Each field allows forces to act without direct contact between objects. Scientists refer to these as noncontact forces. The strength of these forces often depends on factors such as distance, mass, charge, or magnetic properties.

10 Scientists and engineers use their understanding of fields to develop new technologies. Satellites rely on gravity to remain in orbit. Medical equipment uses magnetic fields to create images of structures inside the human body. Electrostatic forces are used in devices such as photocopiers and air-cleaning systems.

11 The world is filled with invisible fields that influence how objects behave. Although people cannot see gravitational, magnetic, or electrostatic fields directly, they can observe their effects. From falling objects to orbiting planets and magnets attracting metal, these fields demonstrate how forces can act across space and change the motion of objects.

title: The Science Behind a Soccer Kick

passages.title: The Science Behind a Soccer Kick
passages.text:
1 During a soccer match, players are constantly applying forces to change the motion of the ball. A ball may sit motionless on the field one moment and race across the grass the next. These changes in motion occur because forces act on the ball.

2 A force is a push or a pull. When a player kicks a soccer ball, the player's foot applies a force to the ball. This force can cause the ball to start moving, speed up, slow down, stop, or change direction. The amount of change depends on the strength and direction of the force.

3 Before a kick, the soccer ball may be at rest. According to Newton's First Law of Motion, an object at rest will remain at rest unless acted upon by an unbalanced force. The player's kick provides that unbalanced force, causing the ball to accelerate.

4 Once the ball is moving, other forces begin to affect its motion. Friction between the ball and the ground gradually slows it down. Air resistance, which is a type of friction between the ball and the air, also opposes motion. Without these forces, the ball would continue moving much farther.

5 Players can control the direction of the ball by changing how they kick it. A straight kick may send the ball directly toward a teammate. A kick from the side can cause the ball to curve through the air. Goalkeepers also apply forces when they catch, block, or deflect the ball.

6 Every pass, shot, and save demonstrates how forces can change motion. Whether a ball starts moving, speeds up, slows down, or changes direction, forces are responsible for each movement seen during a soccer game.

passages.title: Launching a Rocket into Space
passages.text:
1 While the forces involved in a soccer game are easy to observe, the same scientific principles apply to much larger objects. One example is a rocket launch. Sending a spacecraft into orbit requires carefully controlled forces that can overcome Earth's gravity.

2 Before launch, a rocket sits motionless on the launch pad. Gravity pulls downward on the rocket, while the launch pad pushes upward with an equal force. Because these forces are balanced, the rocket remains at rest.

3 At liftoff, engines burn fuel and produce large amounts of hot gas. The gases are expelled downward at high speed. In response, an upward force called thrust acts on the rocket. When the thrust becomes greater than the force of gravity, the rocket begins to accelerate upward.

4 As the rocket climbs, gravity continues to pull downward. Air resistance also opposes the rocket's motion. Engineers must design rockets that produce enough thrust to overcome these opposing forces.

5 Forces continue to affect the rocket after it leaves the atmosphere. Although air resistance becomes very small in space, gravity still acts on the spacecraft. In fact, gravity helps keep satellites and space stations in orbit around Earth.

6 Rocket launches demonstrate how forces can change an object's motion. A rocket that begins at rest can accelerate to thousands of miles per hour because unbalanced forces act upon it. By understanding how forces work, scientists and engineers can safely send spacecraft beyond Earth's surface.

title: Earth's Invisible Pull

passages.title: Earth's Invisible Pull
passages.text:
1 Every day, people experience the effects of gravity, even if they rarely think about it. A dropped pencil falls to the floor, a basketball returns to the ground after a shot, and rain falls from clouds. These events occur because objects are influenced by Earth's gravitational field.

2 A gravitational field is an area around an object where another object experiences a gravitational force. Every object with mass creates a gravitational field. However, larger objects create stronger gravitational fields. Because Earth has far more mass than a pencil or a person, its gravitational field is strong enough to pull nearby objects toward its center.

3 Gravity acts without direct contact. Unlike a push or pull from a person's hand, gravitational force can act across empty space. Scientists classify gravity as a noncontact force because objects do not need to touch for the force to occur.

4 Earth's gravitational field affects much more than objects near the surface. The Moon remains in orbit around Earth because Earth's gravity pulls on it. At the same time, the Moon's gravity affects Earth, causing ocean tides. The Sun's much stronger gravitational field keeps Earth and the other planets moving in their orbits.

5 The strength of gravitational force depends on several factors. One factor is mass. Objects with greater mass create stronger gravitational fields. Another factor is distance. As objects move farther apart, the gravitational force between them becomes weaker.

6 Scientists use their understanding of gravity in many ways. Engineers calculate gravitational forces when designing satellites, spacecraft, and planetary missions. Weather satellites, communication satellites, and the International Space Station all rely on gravity to remain in orbit around Earth.

7 Although gravity is invisible, its effects can be observed throughout the universe. From falling objects on Earth to the motion of planets and moons, gravitational fields continuously exert forces that influence the movement of objects.

passages.title: Magnets and Static Electricity at Work
passages.text:
1 Gravity is not the only force that can act without direct contact. Magnetic and electrostatic fields also exert forces on objects. These invisible fields affect many of the technologies and natural phenomena people encounter every day.

2 A magnetic field surrounds every magnet. Within this field, magnetic forces can attract or repel certain objects. For example, a magnet can pull a paper clip toward it even though the two objects are not touching. The magnetic field exerts a force on the metal object through the space between them.

3 Magnets have two poles, called north and south. Opposite poles attract one another, while like poles repel. This interaction allows magnetic forces to either pull objects together or push them apart. The force becomes weaker as the distance between the magnets increases.

4 Earth's magnetic field also plays an important role. Compasses work because their magnetic needles align with Earth's magnetic field. This allows travelers and explorers to determine direction. Earth's magnetic field also helps protect the planet from charged particles traveling from the Sun.

5 Electrostatic fields create another type of noncontact force. Electrostatic forces result from electric charges. Objects can become positively or negatively charged when electrons transfer from one object to another.

6 A common example occurs when a balloon is rubbed against hair. The rubbing causes electrons to move between the materials, creating an imbalance of charge. The charged balloon may then attract hair, small pieces of paper, or even stick to a wall. These effects occur because an electrostatic field exerts a force on nearby objects.

7 Electrostatic forces can either attract or repel objects. Opposite charges attract one another, while like charges repel. Although these forces are often weaker than gravity on large objects, they can have noticeable effects on small objects and particles.

8 Magnetic and electrostatic fields demonstrate that forces do not always require physical contact. Just like gravity, these fields allow objects to influence one another across space. By studying these invisible forces, scientists and engineers continue to develop technologies that improve everyday life.

title: Fossils: Clues to Life's Changing Story

passages.title: Fossils: Clues to Life's Changing Story
passages.text:
1 Today, millions of different species live on Earth. From tiny bacteria and insects to giant whales and towering trees, life exists in a remarkable variety of forms. Scientists refer to this variety as biodiversity. However, the species alive today are only a small part of Earth's long history. Over billions of years, many species have appeared, changed, and disappeared. Evidence of these changes can be found in the fossil record.

2 A fossil is the preserved evidence of a living organism from the past. Fossils can include bones, teeth, shells, footprints, leaf impressions, and even burrows. Most fossils form when an organism is quickly buried by sediment. Over long periods of time, the sediment hardens into rock, preserving traces of the organism.

3 Fossils provide scientists with valuable information about life that existed long before humans appeared. By studying fossils found in different rock layers, scientists can learn which organisms lived during different periods of Earth's history. Older fossils are generally found in deeper rock layers, while younger fossils are often located closer to the surface.

4 One important pattern revealed by fossils is that the number and types of species have changed over time. Some species that once lived on Earth no longer exist. For example, trilobites, mammoths, and dinosaurs were once common, but these organisms eventually became extinct. Extinction occurs when all members of a species die out.

5 Although extinction may seem unusual, it has occurred many times throughout Earth's history. Changes in climate, competition for resources, natural disasters, and environmental changes can make survival difficult for some species. If a species cannot adapt to changing conditions, it may eventually disappear.

6 At the same time that some species become extinct, new species can develop through the process of evolution. Evolution is the gradual change in populations of organisms over many generations. These changes result from variations in traits among individuals within a species.

7 Traits are inherited characteristics that can be passed from parents to offspring. Individuals within a species often have slight differences in their traits. Some variations may help organisms survive and reproduce more successfully in their environment. Over many generations, these beneficial traits may become more common within the population.

8 Fossil evidence helps scientists observe evolutionary changes. For example, fossils show that ancient horses were much smaller than modern horses and had several toes instead of a single hoof. Over millions of years, horse populations gradually changed as environmental conditions shifted. Fossils preserve evidence of these transitions, allowing scientists to study how species changed through time.

9 The fossil record also shows periods when biodiversity increased. Following some mass extinction events, surviving species evolved into many new forms. As populations adapted to different environments, the number of species grew. This process contributed to the diversity of life observed today.

10 Scientists do not rely on a single fossil to understand Earth's biological history. Instead, they examine thousands of fossils from different locations and time periods. Together, these fossils create a record that reveals patterns of appearance, change, and extinction among species.

11 Although the fossil record is incomplete, it provides strong evidence that life on Earth has changed over time. Fossils show that some species disappeared, others evolved, and new species emerged. Through the combined processes of evolution and extinction, Earth's biodiversity has continually changed across millions of generations.

12 Every fossil represents a small piece of a much larger story. By studying these preserved clues, scientists continue to learn how life has adapted, diversified, and changed throughout Earth's history. The fossil record serves as a window into the past, helping people understand the origins of the incredible diversity of species found on Earth today.

title: A Chain That Stretches Through Time

passages.title: A Chain That Stretches Through Time
passages.text:
1 Every living organism has parents. Those parents had parents of their own, and those parents had parents before them. If someone could trace this pattern backward through time, they would discover an unbroken chain of ancestors stretching back millions of years. Every organism alive today is connected to a long line of ancestors that survived, reproduced, and passed their traits to the next generation.

2 Reproduction is one of the most important characteristics of life. Through reproduction, organisms produce offspring that inherit many of their parents' traits. These inherited traits may include physical characteristics, behaviors, or other features that help organisms survive in their environments.

3 Not every organism survives long enough to reproduce. Throughout history, living things have faced challenges such as predators, disease, competition for resources, and environmental changes. Individuals that survive these challenges and successfully reproduce pass their genes to future generations. As a result, the traits of successful ancestors continue to appear in their descendants.

4 Consider a population of rabbits living in a grassland. Some rabbits may run slightly faster than others. If speed helps rabbits escape predators, faster individuals may be more likely to survive and reproduce. Over many generations, the population may contain more rabbits that inherit traits associated with speed. This process contributes to changes within populations over time.

5 Scientists refer to these inherited changes across generations as evolution. Evolution does not occur within a single individual. Instead, it occurs in populations as traits become more or less common over many generations. The process depends on successful reproduction because inherited traits can only be passed on when organisms produce offspring.

6 Evidence for ancestry can be found in the similarities among living organisms. For example, humans, dogs, whales, and bats appear very different on the outside, yet they share many internal structures. Each has a backbone and similar arrangements of bones in its limbs. These similarities suggest that these organisms share common ancestors from the distant past.

7 Scientists also study DNA to learn about ancestry. DNA contains genetic instructions that are passed from parents to offspring. Organisms that share a recent common ancestor often have more similar DNA than organisms whose common ancestor lived much earlier. By comparing DNA sequences, scientists can identify relationships among species and better understand their evolutionary history.

8 Fossils provide additional evidence about ancestral relationships. Fossils preserve information about organisms that lived long ago. Some fossils show characteristics that are similar to both older and newer species. These fossils help scientists understand how populations changed over time and how different groups of organisms may be connected through ancestry.

9 One example involves modern whales. Fossil evidence suggests that the ancestors of whales once lived on land. Over millions of years, populations gradually changed as they adapted to life in aquatic environments. Fossils reveal a series of organisms with features that link land-dwelling ancestors to the whales living today.

10 The history of life includes countless examples of organisms that successfully reproduced and passed their traits to future generations. At the same time, many other organisms left no descendants because their populations disappeared or became extinct. The species alive today represent only a small portion of all the organisms that have ever lived.

11 Understanding ancestry helps scientists explain both the diversity and the similarities of life on Earth. Although species may appear very different, all living organisms are connected through reproduction and inheritance. Every plant, animal, fungus, and microorganism is part of a long history that extends far into the past.

12 Every organism alive today is the result of an extraordinary chain of successful reproduction. From ancient ancestors that lived millions of years ago to the organisms living today, life has continued because generation after generation survived long enough to produce offspring. The existence of every living thing is evidence of a long line of ancestors connected through time.

title: Why Do Puppies Look Like Their Parents?

passages.title: Why Do Puppies Look Like Their Parents?
passages.text:
1 Anyone who has seen a litter of puppies may notice that the puppies often resemble their parents. Some may have similar fur colors, ear shapes, or body sizes. Although each puppy is unique, many of its characteristics come from traits inherited from its mother and father.

2 Traits are characteristics of an organism. Traits can include physical features such as eye color, fur pattern, height, or leaf shape. Some traits influence behavior, while others affect how an organism functions. Organisms inherit many of these traits through genetic information passed from their parents.

3 Genetic information is stored in DNA, which is found in the cells of living organisms. DNA contains instructions that help determine many of an organism's characteristics. During reproduction, offspring receive DNA from both parents. As a result, offspring often share traits with each parent.

4 Inherited traits can vary among offspring. For example, puppies from the same litter may have different fur colors or markings. This variation occurs because each offspring receives a unique combination of genetic information. Even though siblings share the same parents, they do not inherit exactly the same DNA.

5 Inherited traits are not limited to animals. Plants also receive genetic information from parent plants. The color of a flower, the shape of its leaves, and the size of its fruit are examples of traits that can be passed from one generation to the next.

6 Scientists study inherited traits to better understand how characteristics are passed through populations. By observing similarities between parents and offspring, researchers can identify patterns of inheritance and explain why organisms often resemble their relatives.

7 Although organisms may develop unique characteristics throughout their lives, inherited traits provide the foundation for many of the features they possess. From puppies and kittens to oak trees and sunflowers, living things carry genetic information that links them to their parents.

passages.title: More Than Just Genetics
passages.text:
1 While inherited traits play an important role in determining an organism's characteristics, the environment can also influence how some traits develop. The interaction between inherited traits and environmental conditions helps explain why organisms with similar genetic information may not always look or behave exactly alike.

2 Consider two identical plants grown from the same type of seed. One plant receives plenty of sunlight, water, and nutrients. The other grows in poor soil and receives less sunlight. Although the plants inherited similar genetic information, they may differ in height, leaf size, and overall health because of differences in their environments.

3 Environmental influences can affect animals as well. A young animal may inherit the ability to grow strong muscles, but proper nutrition is necessary for those muscles to develop fully. Similarly, inherited traits may influence an animal's size, but environmental factors such as food availability can affect growth.

4 Some characteristics are almost entirely determined by inherited traits. Blood type in humans is one example. Other characteristics result from a combination of inherited traits and environmental influences. Height, for example, is affected by genetic information as well as nutrition and health during development.

5 Scientists often study twins to better understand the roles of inheritance and environment. Identical twins inherit nearly the same genetic information. When twins grow up in different environments, researchers can observe how environmental factors influence certain characteristics while inherited traits remain similar.

6 Understanding the relationship between inheritance and environment helps scientists explain the diversity seen within populations. Organisms may inherit similar traits from their parents, but environmental conditions can influence how some of those traits are expressed.

7 The characteristics of an organism are closely connected to the traits inherited from its parents. However, environmental factors may also affect how those traits develop. Together, inheritance and environment help shape the unique characteristics of every living thing.

questions.text:
Which statement best describes a difference in emphasis between the two passages?

title: The Rise and Fall of Species

passages.title: The Rise and Fall of Species
passages.text:
1 If someone could travel back through Earth's history, the world would look very different from one time period to another. Strange sea creatures once filled ancient oceans. Giant reptiles roamed the land for millions of years. Many of these organisms no longer exist, while countless new species have appeared. The diversity of life on Earth has changed continuously through the processes of evolution and extinction.

2 A species is a group of organisms that can reproduce and produce offspring. Today, scientists estimate that millions of species live on Earth. This diversity is the result of changes that have occurred over vast periods of time.

3 One reason species change is variation. Individuals within a species often have different traits. Traits are inherited characteristics such as size, color, body structure, or behavior. Some variations may help organisms survive and reproduce more successfully in their environments.

4 Over many generations, beneficial traits can become more common within a population. This gradual process is called evolution. Evolution does not occur in a single organism. Instead, populations change over time as inherited traits are passed from parents to offspring.

5 Consider a population of birds living on an island. Some birds may have slightly larger beaks, while others have smaller beaks. If environmental conditions change and larger beaks make it easier to find food, birds with that trait may survive and reproduce more often. Over many generations, larger beaks may become more common in the population.

6 While evolution can lead to the development of new species, extinction can remove species from Earth. Extinction occurs when all members of a species die out. Many factors can contribute to extinction, including environmental changes, competition, disease, and natural disasters.

7 One of the most famous extinction events occurred about 66 million years ago when a large asteroid struck Earth. The impact caused major environmental changes that contributed to the extinction of many species, including most dinosaurs. However, some groups of organisms survived and later evolved into new forms.

8 Evolution and extinction work together to shape biodiversity. As some species disappear, others emerge and diversify. The species living today represent only a small fraction of all the organisms that have existed throughout Earth's history.

9 Scientists study these processes to better understand how life changes over time. Evidence from fossils helps reveal when species appeared, changed, diversified, or became extinct. By examining this evidence, researchers can reconstruct Earth's biological history and explain how modern biodiversity developed.

passages.title: Fossils Tell the Story
passages.text:
1 Imagine finding a fossil shell embedded in a rock far from any ocean. That single discovery could provide important clues about Earth's past. Fossils serve as records of organisms that lived long ago and help scientists understand how the number and types of species have changed over time.

2 Fossils are preserved remains, traces, or impressions of ancient organisms. They may include bones, teeth, shells, footprints, or even impressions of leaves. Most fossils form when organisms are buried by sediment that later hardens into rock.

3 Because different rock layers formed during different periods of time, fossils found within those layers provide information about past life. Older fossils are generally found in deeper layers, while younger fossils are usually found closer to the surface. This arrangement helps scientists place organisms in a timeline of Earth's history.

4 The fossil record reveals that many species once existed but are now extinct. Fossils of trilobites, mammoths, saber-toothed cats, and dinosaurs provide evidence of organisms that lived successfully for long periods before eventually disappearing. Without fossils, scientists might never know these species existed.

5 Fossils also provide evidence of evolutionary change. In some cases, scientists have discovered sequences of fossils showing gradual changes in organisms over time. For example, fossils of ancient horses reveal that early horse ancestors were much smaller than modern horses and had several toes instead of a single hoof.

6 Fossil evidence shows that Earth's biodiversity has not remained constant. Some periods in history experienced rapid increases in the number of species, while others experienced major extinction events that reduced biodiversity. These patterns demonstrate that life on Earth has changed repeatedly through time.

7 The fossil record is not perfect. Many organisms never became fossilized, and some fossils have been destroyed by erosion, heat, or pressure. Despite these gaps, scientists have collected millions of fossils from around the world. Together, these discoveries provide strong evidence that species have evolved, diversified, and become extinct throughout Earth's history.

8 Every fossil represents a small piece of a much larger story. When scientists combine fossil evidence from different locations and time periods, they gain a clearer understanding of how life has changed over hundreds of millions of years. The fossil record continues to reveal new information about the ever-changing diversity of species on Earth.

title: Rebuilding a Nation: The Challenges of Reconstruction

passages.title: Rebuilding a Nation: The Challenges of Reconstruction
passages.text:
1 In April 1865, the American Civil War came to an end. After four years of conflict, the United States faced the difficult task of rebuilding a divided nation. Much of the South's economy and infrastructure had been damaged, millions of formerly enslaved people were seeking new opportunities, and questions remained about how the former Confederate states would rejoin the Union. The period that followed the Civil War became known as Reconstruction.

2 Reconstruction lasted from 1865 to 1877. During these years, government leaders worked to restore the nation and address issues that had contributed to the war. The federal government played a major role in shaping policies for the South and determining how the country would move forward.

3 One of the most significant results of Reconstruction was a series of changes to the United States Constitution. These changes, known as the Reconstruction Amendments, expanded rights and redefined the relationship between citizens and the government.

4 The Thirteenth Amendment, ratified in 1865, abolished slavery throughout the United States. Although the Emancipation Proclamation had freed many enslaved people during the war, the amendment permanently ended slavery nationwide. For millions of African Americans, this marked the beginning of a new chapter in American history.

5 Three years later, the Fourteenth Amendment became part of the Constitution. This amendment granted citizenship to all people born or naturalized in the United States and guaranteed equal protection under the law. The amendment increased the federal government's responsibility for protecting the rights of citizens.

6 In 1870, the Fifteenth Amendment was ratified. It prohibited states from denying citizens the right to vote based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude. Together, the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Amendments significantly expanded civil rights and altered the Constitution in ways that continue to influence American society today.

7 Reconstruction also strengthened federal authority. Before the Civil War, disagreements often arose over the balance of power between state governments and the federal government. After the war, federal leaders took a more active role in protecting civil rights and overseeing the process of rebuilding the South.

8 To help formerly enslaved people transition to freedom, Congress established the Freedmen's Bureau in 1865. The agency provided assistance such as food, education, medical care, and legal support. The federal government's involvement in these efforts demonstrated its growing role in addressing national issues.

9 Despite these changes, Reconstruction was marked by significant challenges and disagreements. Many Southern states resisted efforts to expand rights for African Americans. Some state governments passed laws known as Black Codes, which restricted the freedoms of formerly enslaved people and limited their economic and political opportunities.

10 In response, Congress passed legislation designed to protect civil rights and enforce the new constitutional amendments. However, conflicts continued as different groups debated the extent of federal authority and the pace of social change. These disagreements often reflected deeper tensions that remained from the Civil War.

11 Political differences also shaped the Reconstruction era. Some Americans believed the federal government should take strong action to ensure equal rights and rebuild Southern society. Others argued that states should have greater control over their own affairs. These debates influenced elections, laws, and government policies throughout the period.

12 Social divisions remained as well. Although the Reconstruction Amendments established important legal protections, many African Americans continued to face discrimination and obstacles to exercising their rights. In some areas, violence and intimidation were used to discourage political participation and maintain existing social structures.

13 By 1877, Reconstruction came to an end as federal troops were withdrawn from the South. Many of the goals of Reconstruction remained unfinished. While the constitutional amendments continued to provide a foundation for future civil rights efforts, social and political conflicts persisted for decades.

14 Reconstruction transformed the United States in important ways. The Constitution was amended to expand rights and citizenship, the authority of the federal government was strengthened, and efforts were made to rebuild a divided nation. At the same time, lingering social and political differences demonstrated that healing the effects of the Civil War would be a long and difficult process.

15 The legacy of Reconstruction can still be seen today. The amendments adopted during this period remain part of the Constitution, and debates about civil rights, citizenship, and the role of government continue to shape American society. Understanding Reconstruction helps explain how the nation attempted to rebuild after one of the most challenging periods in its history.

title: Turning Points of the Civil War

passages.title: Turning Points of the Civil War
passages.text:
1 The American Civil War, fought from 1861 to 1865, was one of the most significant conflicts in United States history. The war began after long-standing disagreements between the Northern and Southern states over issues including slavery, states' rights, and political power. As the conflict unfolded, key events and influential leaders helped shape its course and determine its outcome.

2 When the war began in April 1861, many people believed the fighting would end quickly. Instead, the conflict lasted four years and became the deadliest war in American history. Both the Union, representing the Northern states, and the Confederacy, representing the Southern states, faced difficult challenges as they sought victory.

3 One of the most important figures in the war was President Abraham Lincoln. As leader of the Union, Lincoln worked to preserve the United States and prevent the nation from dividing permanently. Throughout the war, he made critical decisions regarding military strategy, political leadership, and the future of slavery in the country.

4 Early in the conflict, the Union experienced both successes and setbacks. Several major battles demonstrated that neither side would achieve an easy victory. The First Battle of Bull Run in 1861 shocked many Americans because it revealed how long and costly the war might become.

5 Military leaders also played important roles in shaping the war's outcome. General Ulysses S. Grant became one of the Union's most successful commanders. Grant's willingness to continue fighting despite heavy losses helped the Union gain important victories in the western part of the country. His leadership eventually earned him command of all Union armies.

6 On the Confederate side, General Robert E. Lee emerged as one of the most respected military leaders of the war. Lee led the Army of Northern Virginia and won several impressive victories against larger Union forces. His leadership allowed the Confederacy to remain a powerful opponent for much of the conflict.

7 Another turning point occurred in 1863 when Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation. This executive order declared that enslaved people in areas controlled by the Confederacy were free. Although it did not immediately end slavery everywhere, the proclamation changed the purpose of the war. In addition to preserving the Union, the conflict became a struggle against slavery.

8 The Emancipation Proclamation also had important military and diplomatic effects. Thousands of African Americans joined the Union Army and Navy after the proclamation was issued. Their contributions strengthened Union forces. In addition, the proclamation discouraged some foreign nations from supporting the Confederacy because many countries had already abolished slavery.

9 Another key event occurred during the summer of 1863 at the Battle of Gettysburg. Fought in Pennsylvania, Gettysburg became the largest battle of the war. After three days of intense fighting, Confederate forces under General Lee retreated to the South. Historians often view Gettysburg as a major turning point because it ended Lee's attempt to invade the North and shifted momentum toward the Union.

10 Later that same year, Union forces achieved another important victory at Vicksburg, Mississippi. Control of Vicksburg gave the Union command of the Mississippi River, a vital transportation route. This victory divided the Confederacy and made it more difficult for Southern states to coordinate resources and military operations.

11 As the war continued, the Union's advantages in population, industry, transportation, and resources became increasingly important. Northern factories produced weapons, ammunition, clothing, and supplies that supported the war effort. Railroads helped move soldiers and materials efficiently across large distances.

12 In 1864, General William Tecumseh Sherman led a campaign through Georgia that further weakened the Confederacy. Sherman's forces captured Atlanta and later marched to the Atlantic Ocean. His campaign damaged transportation networks, factories, and other resources that supported the Confederate war effort.

13 By early 1865, Confederate armies faced growing shortages of soldiers and supplies. Union forces continued to advance, placing increasing pressure on Southern leaders. In April 1865, General Lee surrendered to General Grant at Appomattox Court House in Virginia, effectively ending the war.

14 The Civil War had a lasting impact on the United States. The Union was preserved, slavery was abolished through the Thirteenth Amendment, and the nation entered the Reconstruction era. Many of the political, economic, and social issues that influenced the war continued to shape American history long afterward.

15 The outcome of the Civil War was influenced by a combination of leadership, military strategy, and significant events. Figures such as Abraham Lincoln, Ulysses S. Grant, and Robert E. Lee played important roles, while events including the Emancipation Proclamation, the Battle of Gettysburg, and the capture of Vicksburg helped determine the course of the conflict. Together, these people and events shaped one of the most important chapters in American history.

title: A Nation Divided by Power

passages.title: A Nation Divided by Power
passages.text:
1 During the first half of the nineteenth century, the United States experienced rapid growth and change. New states joined the Union, transportation networks expanded, and the economy developed in different ways across regions of the country. As the nation grew, disagreements emerged about the balance of power between the federal government and state governments. These disputes became a major source of tension between the North and the South.

2 The United States operates under a system known as federalism. In a federal system, power is shared between a national government and state governments. Although the Constitution outlines many powers of the federal government, disagreements often arise over where federal authority ends and state authority begins.

3 Throughout the early 1800s, many political leaders debated the proper role of the federal government. Some believed the federal government should have broad powers to regulate commerce, build infrastructure, and address national issues. Others argued that states should retain greater authority to make decisions for themselves.

4 These debates became more complicated as the nation's economy developed. In the North, manufacturing and industry expanded rapidly. Factories produced goods, cities grew, and railroads connected markets. Northern leaders often supported federal policies that encouraged economic growth, including investments in transportation and protective tariffs on imported goods.

5 In contrast, the Southern economy depended heavily on agriculture. Large plantations produced crops such as cotton, tobacco, and rice. Many Southern leaders believed federal policies, especially tariffs, benefited Northern industries more than Southern farmers. As a result, some Southerners favored stronger state authority and a more limited federal government.

6 One example of these tensions occurred during the Nullification Crisis of the early 1830s. South Carolina argued that states had the right to reject federal laws they considered unconstitutional. President Andrew Jackson disagreed and defended federal authority. Although the crisis was resolved peacefully, it revealed deep disagreements about federalism that would continue for decades.

7 As new territories were added to the United States, another question emerged: Would these territories permit slavery? The answer would affect political power in Congress and further increase sectional tensions. The issue of slavery soon became closely connected to debates about federal and state authority.

8 By the mid-1800s, many Americans increasingly identified with their region rather than the nation as a whole. Differences in economic interests and beliefs about government power contributed to growing sectional divisions between the North and the South.

passages.title: Slavery and the Road to Civil War
passages.text:
1 While disputes over federalism and economic development created tensions, slavery became the most divisive issue facing the United States. As the nation expanded westward, disagreements over whether slavery would be allowed in new territories intensified sectional conflict.

2 In 1820, Congress attempted to address these disagreements through the Missouri Compromise. The agreement admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state while establishing a line that limited the expansion of slavery in certain western territories. Although the compromise temporarily eased tensions, it did not resolve the underlying conflict.

3 Over the following decades, new compromises and laws attempted to manage the issue. The Compromise of 1850 and the Kansas-Nebraska Act addressed the question of slavery in newly acquired territories. However, these measures often increased disagreement rather than reducing it.

4 In Kansas, supporters and opponents of slavery rushed to influence local elections and determine whether the territory would permit slavery. The violence that followed earned the region the nickname "Bleeding Kansas." The conflict demonstrated how deeply divided the nation had become.

5 The debate over slavery also expanded into the courts. In 1857, the Supreme Court issued its decision in the Dred Scott case. The Court ruled that Congress could not prohibit slavery in federal territories. Many Northerners opposed the decision, while many Southerners supported it. The ruling further increased sectional tensions.

6 At the same time, abolitionists worked to end slavery. Individuals such as Frederick Douglass, Harriet Tubman, and Sojourner Truth spoke against slavery and encouraged reform. Their efforts helped bring greater national attention to the issue.

7 The election of Abraham Lincoln in 1860 became another turning point. Although Lincoln promised not to interfere with slavery where it already existed, many Southern leaders feared that his administration would limit the institution's future expansion. Following his election, several Southern states voted to secede from the Union.

8 Southern leaders argued that states possessed the authority to leave the Union. Many Northern leaders rejected this claim and maintained that the Union should remain intact. The disagreement reflected long-standing disputes about the nature of federalism and the relationship between state and federal power.

9 In April 1861, Confederate forces fired on Fort Sumter in South Carolina. The attack marked the beginning of the American Civil War. What had started as decades of disagreements over federalism, economic interests, sectional identity, and slavery had developed into a national conflict.

10 The Civil War would ultimately decide questions that had divided the nation for generations. The conflict demonstrated how disputes over government authority, economic development, and slavery had combined to shape one of the most significant events in American history.

title: Westward Expansion and a Growing Nation

passages.title: Westward Expansion and a Growing Nation
passages.text:
1 During the nineteenth century, millions of Americans looked westward in search of land, opportunity, and resources. As settlers moved across the continent, the United States expanded its territory and strengthened its economy. Roads, railroads, farms, and towns spread into new regions, transforming both the landscape and the nation.

2 One idea that encouraged westward movement was Manifest Destiny. Many Americans believed that the United States was destined to expand across North America from the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean. Supporters of Manifest Destiny viewed expansion as both an opportunity and a responsibility.

3 As settlers moved west, agriculture expanded rapidly. Farmers cultivated large areas of land and produced crops that could be sold throughout the country. Improvements in farming technology increased productivity and helped support a growing population.

4 Transportation developments also contributed to economic growth. The construction of canals, roads, and railroads connected distant regions and allowed goods to move more efficiently. Farmers in western territories could ship crops to eastern cities, while manufactured goods from factories could reach western communities.

5 Industrial development benefited from westward expansion as well. The West contained valuable natural resources, including timber, coal, gold, silver, and iron ore. These resources supplied growing industries and contributed to the nation's economic development.

6 The discovery of gold in California in 1848 attracted thousands of people seeking wealth. The California Gold Rush led to rapid population growth and increased economic activity. Businesses, transportation systems, and new communities developed to support the growing population.

7 As new territories joined the United States, leaders debated how these regions should be governed. Questions arose about transportation, land ownership, and political representation. The addition of new territories also raised difficult questions about whether slavery would be allowed to expand into western lands.

8 By the mid-1800s, westward expansion had become one of the most important forces shaping the nation. While expansion created economic opportunities and encouraged industrial growth, it also contributed to disagreements that would have lasting consequences for the United States.

passages.title: Conflict and Consequences in the West
passages.text:
1 Although westward expansion created opportunities for many Americans, it also led to significant conflicts. Debates over slavery, war with Mexico, and the displacement of American Indians became major issues as the nation expanded.

2 One source of conflict involved the expansion of slavery. As new territories were acquired, Americans disagreed about whether slavery should be permitted in those regions. Northern and Southern leaders recognized that each new state's decision could affect the balance of political power in Congress.

3 These disagreements contributed to growing sectional tensions between the North and South. Efforts such as the Missouri Compromise and later the Compromise of 1850 attempted to address the issue, but debates over slavery continued as the nation expanded westward.

4 Westward expansion also contributed to war with Mexico. In 1845, the United States annexed Texas, which had previously gained independence from Mexico. Disputes over the Texas border and other territorial issues increased tensions between the two nations.

5 In 1846, the Mexican-American War began. After nearly two years of fighting, the United States defeated Mexico. The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo ended the war in 1848 and added a vast amount of territory to the United States, including lands that would later become California, Nevada, Utah, and parts of several other states.

6 While many Americans celebrated the territorial gains, others worried about how the new lands would affect the debate over slavery. The question of whether slavery would expand into the territories acquired from Mexico became a major political issue.

7 American Indians were also deeply affected by westward expansion. For thousands of years, Indigenous peoples had lived throughout North America. As settlers moved west, increasing numbers of American Indians were forced from their homelands.

8 In 1830, Congress passed the Indian Removal Act. The law authorized the relocation of many tribes living east of the Mississippi River. Thousands of Cherokee people and members of other tribes were forced to travel westward under difficult conditions in what became known as the Trail of Tears.

9 Even after removal, conflicts continued as settlers moved into lands occupied by American Indian nations. Competition for land, resources, and control of territory led to additional displacement and loss of traditional homelands for many Indigenous groups.

10 Westward expansion transformed the United States in many ways. It contributed to economic and industrial development, increased the nation's territory, and created new opportunities for settlement. At the same time, it intensified sectional debates, led to war with Mexico, and caused the displacement of many American Indians. These consequences continue to influence how Americans understand this important period of history.

title: One Nation, Many Voices

passages.title: One Nation, Many Voices
passages.text:
1 In the years following the American Revolution, the United States faced a difficult challenge. The new nation stretched across a vast area and included people from different regions, cultures, religions, and backgrounds. Although Americans shared a desire for self-government, they often differed in their customs, economies, and daily lives. Over time, however, many Americans began to develop a unique national identity based on common democratic ideals.

2 During the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries, regional differences were easy to see. In New England, many people worked in shipping, trade, and small industries. In the South, agriculture dominated the economy, with many large plantations producing crops such as tobacco and cotton. Farmers in the western territories focused on clearing land and building new communities. Despite these differences, Americans increasingly viewed themselves as members of a single nation.

3 One factor that helped unite Americans was the Constitution. Ratified in 1788, the Constitution established a framework for government based on democratic principles. Citizens elected representatives, laws applied across the nation, and power was divided among branches of government. Although Americans often disagreed about specific policies, many supported the ideals of representative government and individual rights.

4 The Bill of Rights further strengthened these democratic ideals. The first ten amendments to the Constitution protected freedoms such as speech, religion, press, assembly, and petition. These rights became important parts of American identity and reflected the belief that citizens should have a voice in government.

5 As the nation grew, important events encouraged feelings of national unity. The War of 1812, fought between the United States and Great Britain, helped many Americans develop a stronger sense of national pride. Although the war produced mixed military results, Americans celebrated their ability to defend their independence. Symbols such as the American flag and patriotic songs became increasingly important during this period.

6 Improvements in transportation and communication also helped connect different parts of the country. New roads, canals, and steamboats made travel easier and allowed goods and information to move more quickly. Newspapers spread news across the nation, helping people in distant regions learn about shared events and issues.

7 Democratic participation expanded during the early nineteenth century as more states removed property requirements for voting. As a result, more white men gained the right to vote and participate in elections. Many Americans viewed increased political participation as evidence that democratic ideals were becoming more deeply rooted in the nation.

8 At the same time, reform movements emerged as citizens sought to improve society. Some Americans worked to expand educational opportunities, improve working conditions, or address social problems. Others joined movements that called for the abolition of slavery or greater rights for women. Although reformers often disagreed about specific goals, many believed that citizens had a responsibility to help create a better society.

9 American culture also contributed to the development of a national identity. Writers, artists, and musicians began creating works that reflected uniquely American experiences. Authors described frontier life, democratic values, and the nation's landscapes. Their work helped Americans see their country as distinct from European nations.

10 Despite growing national unity, important divisions remained. Regional disagreements over economic policies, states' rights, and slavery continued to create tension. Immigrants arriving from many countries brought new traditions and perspectives. Native American nations maintained their own cultures and identities, even as westward expansion transformed the continent.

11 These differences meant that American identity was not based on a single culture or tradition. Instead, it developed through a combination of shared political ideals and diverse experiences. People from different regions and backgrounds often disagreed, but many embraced principles such as liberty, representation, and participation in government.

12 Over time, democratic ideals became a powerful force in shaping how Americans viewed themselves and their nation. Citizens celebrated freedoms protected by the Constitution and participated in a growing democratic system. These experiences helped create a sense of national identity that connected people across regional and cultural differences.

13 The development of an American identity was an ongoing process. It emerged from the interaction of diverse populations, shared experiences, and common beliefs about government and citizenship. Although challenges and disagreements persisted, democratic ideals provided a foundation that helped unite many Americans as members of a single nation.

14 By the middle of the nineteenth century, Americans had developed a stronger sense of national identity than had existed at the nation's founding. While regional and cultural differences remained important, many citizens increasingly viewed themselves not only as residents of a state or region but also as Americans united by democratic principles and a shared commitment to self-government.
